definition: A method that has grown too large, mixing multiple steps and decisions so it becomes hard to read, test, and safely change.
ruleOfThumb: If you need to scroll to understand one method, split it into named steps.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: What is the main maintainability problem in this function?

options.text: The function combines pricing, validation, inventory updates, payment, notifications, and logging in one place, so any small change requires understanding the whole flow
options.explanation: This function packs several distinct steps into one block. A change to emails, payment, or inventory forces developers to re-parse unrelated logic, increasing bug risk.

options.text: The tax rate should come from configuration instead of being hardcoded
options.explanation: That is a valid improvement, but it is not the core structural issue. Even with configurable tax, the function is still overloaded with too many concerns.

options.text: Calling reduce on cart items is slower than using a for loop
options.explanation: Micro-performance is not the central concern here. Readability and change-safety are the main problems.

options.text: Returning paymentId leaks implementation details to callers
options.explanation: Return shape can be debated, but it does not explain why this function is difficult to maintain. The issue is too much behavior in one method.

questions.prompt: A bug appears in tax calculation and another in email copy. Why is this method expensive to fix safely?

options.text: Both fixes land in one high-traffic method, so touching tax logic risks side effects in persistence, analytics, or email behavior
options.explanation: When one method orchestrates many steps, even localized edits require broad regression confidence. Separate named steps or smaller collaborators reduce this blast radius.

options.text: The class violates dependency injection because repo and mailer are accessed directly
options.explanation: DI may or may not be used behind the scenes; this snippet does not prove that issue. The visible problem is method size and breadth of responsibilities.

options.text: The method should return void because invoices should be fetched from the database
options.explanation: Return style is an API design choice, not the core maintainability risk shown here.

options.text: Tracking analytics inside business code is always an anti-pattern
options.explanation: Analytics can be acceptable depending on architecture. The bigger issue is that this one method keeps accumulating unrelated steps.

questions.prompt: Both versions generate and send invoices. Which version is more likely to become brittle as features are added?

options.text: Version A — the flow keeps accumulating concrete steps inline, so each new requirement extends an already crowded method
options.explanation: Version A is still readable now, but it grows linearly as responsibilities are added. Version B uses higher-level steps, creating places to extract detail without inflating run().

options.text: Version B — delegating to helper methods hides complexity and makes debugging impossible
options.explanation: Naming and delegation are key readability tools. Debugging becomes easier when each step has a clear purpose and narrower scope.
